channel.title: Voidmath Terms on emsenn.net
channel.description: Recent content in Voidmath Terms on emsenn.net

item.title: Bilattice homomorphism
item.description:
A bilattice homomorphism is a function h:B1→B2h : \mathbf{B}_1 \to \mathbf{B}_2 between bilattices preserving all four lattice operations and the negation:

  • h(a∧tb)=h(a)∧th(b)h(a \wedge_t b) = h(a) \wedge_t h(b) and h(a∨tb)=h(a)∨th(b)h(a \vee_t b) = h(a) \vee_t h(b);
  • h(a∧kb)=h(a)∧kh(b)h(a \wedge_k b) = h(a) \wedge_k h(b) and h(a∨kb)=h(a)∨kh(b)h(a \vee_k b) = h(a) \vee_k h(b);
  • h(∼a)=∼h(a)h(\sim a) = \sim h(a).

When the bilattices carry a conflation, a bilattice homomorphism is additionally required to preserve it: h(−a)=−h(a)h(-a) = -h(a).

A bilattice homomorphism need not preserve bounds. A homomorphism that additionally preserves the knowledge-order bottom — h(⊥)=⊥h(\bot) = \bot — is called bottom-preserving.

item.title: Closure operator
item.description:
A closure operator on the predication fiber functor F:Cop→DBiLat(L)F : \mathcal{C}^{\mathrm{op}} \to \mathbf{DBiLat}(\mathbf{L}) is a family of maps cl=(clp)p∈C\mathrm{cl} = (\mathrm{cl}_p)_{p \in \mathcal{C}}, one per predicate, with clp:Ap→Ap\mathrm{cl}_p : A_p \to A_p on each predication fiber algebra, satisfying four axioms:

  1. Monotone in ≤k\leq_k: v≤kv′⇒clp(v)≤kclp(v′)v \leq_k v' \Rightarrow \mathrm{cl}_p(v) \leq_k \mathrm{cl}_p(v') in the knowledge order.
  2. Inflationary: v≤kclp(v)v \leq_k \mathrm{cl}_p(v).
  3. Idempotent: clp∘clp=clp\mathrm{cl}_p \circ \mathrm{cl}_p = \mathrm{cl}_p.
  4. Natural in C\mathcal{C}: for every morphism f:q→pf : q \to p in the predicate category C\mathcal{C}, f∗∘clp=clq∘f∗.f^* \circ \mathrm{cl}_p = \mathrm{cl}_q \circ f^*.

Conditions (1)–(3) are the classical lattice-theoretic closure axioms applied per fiber: each clp\mathrm{cl}_p is a closure operator on the knowledge-order lattice (Ap,≤k)(A_p, \leq_k) in the standard sense. Condition (4) is the additional content imposed by the family structure: the closures at distinct predicates are coherent under reindexing — closing then reindexing equals reindexing then closing.

item.title: Conflation
item.description:
A conflation −- on a bilattice is a unary operation satisfying three axioms — the duals of negation’s axioms with the two parallel orders exchanged:

  • Antitone in the knowledge order: a≤kb⇒−b≤k−aa \leq_k b \Rightarrow -b \leq_k -a.
  • Monotone in the truth order: a≤tb⇒−a≤t−ba \leq_t b \Rightarrow -a \leq_t -b.
  • Involutive: −(−a)=a-(-a) = a.

Conflation is optional — a bilattice need not have a conflation. When present, it is the second involution alongside negation, and the two are typically required to commute:

item.title: Entity
item.description:
An entity is an element of an entity set, denoted ee. Entities are the individuals or nodes that predicates discuss. Each entity is just an element of a set; the entity itself carries no required structure beyond set membership.

In the predication framework, every entity may occupy the subject or object position of a predication cell. The first and third coordinates of a cell (s,p,o)(s, p, o) are entities; the cell records that predicate pp holds of subject ss in relation to object oo. The same entity may occupy the subject coordinate of one cell and the object coordinate of another; being an entity is not specific to any role within a particular cell.

item.title: Entity set
item.description:
An entity set, denoted EE, is a set whose elements are entities. The entity set is one of the base parameters of the predication framework, alongside a predicate set Π\Pi (the object set of a predicate category) and a residuated lattice L\mathbf{L} from which the bilattice is built.

The entity set carries no required structure beyond set-hood in general. Specific theorems and applications may impose additional structure:

  • Finiteness. Many constructions (the predicate-category generator-exhaustiveness theorem, the existence of certain limits, decidable settlement checks) require EE to be finite, often with ∣E∣≥3|E| \geq 3 for the equivariance hypothesis to be non-degenerate.
  • Sortedness. EE may be sorted into kinds: E=⨆iEiE = \bigsqcup_i E_i where each EiE_i is a sub-set of entities of one type. Sortedness affects which predicates apply to which entity pairs.
  • Group action. The framework’s Sym(E)\mathrm{Sym}(E)-equivariance hypothesis treats EE as a set acted on by its symmetric group Sym(E)\mathrm{Sym}(E); this is what makes the four predicate-category generator classes exhaustive (under ∣E∣≥3|E| \geq 3).

¶The cell index

The Cartesian product E×EE \times E is the cell index — the set of pairs over which predication fiber algebras are built. For each predicate pp, the fiber algebra ApA_p assigns a value to each pair (s,o)∈E×E(s, o) \in E \times E, where ss is the subject and oo is the object of the resulting predication cell.

item.title: Evidence
item.description:
Evidence is the role borne by the two coordinates of a twist-product value ⟨a+,a−⟩∈L×L\langle a_+, a_- \rangle \in L \times L. The first coordinate a+a_+ tracks positive evidence — information for the assertion. The second coordinate a−a_- tracks negative evidence — information against. Together they record the two-sided evidential situation a value reports.

The two evidence kinds are not just two lattice components used as bookkeeping. They behave differently under the two parallel orders of the bilattice, and the difference is what makes the framework’s settlement structure four-cornered rather than bivalent.

item.title: Four-bilattice
item.description:
The four-element bilattice, denoted FOUR\mathbf{FOUR}, is the smallest non-trivial bilattice. Its carrier is

{t,f,⊥,⊤}\{\mathbf{t}, \mathbf{f}, \bot, \top\}

and its truth order, knowledge order, and negation make it a complete bilattice with all the structure voidmath uses.

This bilattice was introduced by Belnap (1977) as a four-valued logic for reasoning under incomplete and contradictory information, and recognized by Ginsberg (1988) and Fitting (1991) as the canonical small example of the bilattice structure later generalized.

¶The two orders

The truth order ≤t\leq_t is generated by:

item.title: Negation
item.description:
A negation ∼\sim on a bilattice is a unary operation satisfying three axioms:

  • Antitone in the truth order: a≤tb⇒∼b≤t∼aa \leq_t b \Rightarrow \sim b \leq_t \sim a.
  • Monotone in the knowledge order: a≤kb⇒∼a≤k∼ba \leq_k b \Rightarrow \sim a \leq_k \sim b.
  • Involutive: ∼∼a=a\sim \sim a = a.

A pre-bilattice equipped with a negation is a bilattice — negation is the structure that distinguishes a full bilattice from the bare-bones two-order pre-bilattice.

¶On twist products: evidence swap

When the bilattice is a twist product L⋈\mathbf{L}^\bowtie — the construction that produces voidmath’s bilattice from a residuated lattice — negation acts by swapping the two coordinates:

item.title: Pre-bilattice
item.description:
A pre-bilattice is an algebra B=⟨B,∧t,∨t,∧k,∨k⟩\mathbf{B} = \langle B, \wedge_t, \vee_t, \wedge_k, \vee_k \rangle in which both ⟨B,∧t,∨t⟩\langle B, \wedge_t, \vee_t \rangle and ⟨B,∧k,∨k⟩\langle B, \wedge_k, \vee_k \rangle are lattices on the common carrier BB.

The order associated with the first lattice is the truth order ≤t\leq_t; the order associated with the second is the knowledge order ≤k\leq_k. When both lattices are bounded, their extremes are denoted f,t\mathbf{f}, \mathbf{t} (bottom and top in ≤t\leq_t) and ⊥,⊤\bot, \top (bottom and top in ≤k\leq_k).

item.title: Predicate
item.description:
A predicate, denoted pp, is an element of the predicate set Π\Pi — equivalently, an object of the predicate category C\mathcal{C}, since Π=Ob(C)\Pi = \mathrm{Ob}(\mathcal{C}). A predicate occupies the second coordinate of a predication cell: in the triple (s,p,o)(s, p, o), the predicate pp is the relation that holds of subject ss in relation to object oo.

¶The directional element

The predicate is what makes a cell directional. The cell’s two entity coordinates (subject and object) could in principle be swapped — but doing so produces a different cell, with the predicate’s converse pcp^c standing where pp stood. Whether pp is symmetric (equal to its own converse) or asymmetric is a property of pp itself, encoded by which morphisms of the predicate category act trivially on it.

item.title: Predicate converse
item.description:
A predicate converse is a morphism in the predicate category, denoted f:pc→pf : p^c \to p, induced by the swap of the entity-pair index:

(s,o)↦(o,s).(s, o) \mapsto (o, s).

The induced fiber-algebra map f∗:Ap→Apcf^* : A_p \to A_{p^c} acts cellwise:

f∗(v)(s,o)=v(o,s).f^*(v)(s, o) = v(o, s).

A predicate converse encodes the relational converse of pp: the predicate pcp^c holds at the pair (s,o)(s, o) exactly where pp holds at (o,s)(o, s). It is one of the four canonical generator classes of the predicate category under Sym(E)\mathrm{Sym}(E)-equivariance.

item.title: Predicate object-projection
item.description:
A predicate object-projection is a morphism in the predicate category, denoted f:q→pf : q \to p, induced by the collapse of the entity-pair index to the object diagonal:

(s,o)↦(o,o).(s, o) \mapsto (o, o).

The induced fiber-algebra map f∗:Ap→Aqf^* : A_p \to A_q acts cellwise:

f∗(v)(s,o)=v(o,o).f^*(v)(s, o) = v(o, o).

The resulting predicate qq has values that depend only on pp’s object-diagonal content: at every cell (s,o)(s, o), qq inherits whatever value pp takes at (o,o)(o, o). The subject coordinate ss becomes irrelevant — it doesn’t enter the definition of qq’s value at all. Object-projection is the dual of subject-projection, and one of the four canonical generator classes of the predicate category.

item.title: Predicate set
item.description:
A predicate set, denoted Π\Pi, is the object set of a predicate category C\mathcal{C}:

Π:=Ob(C).\Pi := \mathrm{Ob}(\mathcal{C}).

The elements of the predicate set are predicates. The predicate set is one of the base parameters of the predication framework; together with the entity set EE and the residuated lattice L\mathbf{L} from which the bilattice is built, it determines the framework’s basic data.

¶Set vs. category

The predicate set is the underlying set of the predicate category — the bare collection of predicates without the morphisms that relate them. The categorical structure (specialization, converse, the two projections) is layered on top of the set; many definitions and constructions can refer to the set alone when the category structure is not at issue.

item.title: Predicate specialization
item.description:
A predicate specialization is a morphism in the predicate category, denoted f:q→pf : q \to p, given by a sub-support inclusion

ιf:dom(q)↪dom(p)\iota_f : \mathrm{dom}(q) \hookrightarrow \mathrm{dom}(p)

where dom(p),dom(q)⊆E×E\mathrm{dom}(p), \mathrm{dom}(q) \subseteq E \times E are structurally designated sub-supports attached to pp and qq within the entity set EE.

The induced fiber-algebra map f∗:Ap→Aqf^* : A_p \to A_q is restriction along ιf\iota_f, extending by ⊥\bot outside the smaller domain:

f∗(v)(s,o)={v(s,o)if (s,o)∈dom(q)⊥otherwise.f^*(v)(s, o) = \begin{cases} v(s, o) & \text{if } (s, o) \in \mathrm{dom}(q) \\ \bot & \text{otherwise.} \end{cases}

A specialization encodes that qq is a sub-predicate of pp: qq’s cells are a structurally designated subset of pp’s cells, with qq’s values inherited from pp’s on that subset and extended by ⊥\bot outside. Specialization is one of the four canonical generator classes of the predicate category.

item.title: Predicate subject-projection
item.description:
A predicate subject-projection is a morphism in the predicate category, denoted f:q→pf : q \to p, induced by the collapse of the entity-pair index to the subject diagonal:

(s,o)↦(s,s).(s, o) \mapsto (s, s).

The induced fiber-algebra map f∗:Ap→Aqf^* : A_p \to A_q acts cellwise:

f∗(v)(s,o)=v(s,s).f^*(v)(s, o) = v(s, s).

The resulting predicate qq has values that depend only on pp’s subject-diagonal content: at every cell (s,o)(s, o), qq inherits whatever value pp takes at (s,s)(s, s). The object coordinate oo becomes irrelevant — it doesn’t enter the definition of qq’s value at all. Subject-projection is one of the four canonical generator classes of the predicate category.

item.title: Predication cell set
item.description:
The predication cell set, denoted Φ\Phi, is the Cartesian product of an entity set EE, a predicate set Π\Pi, and the entity set EE again:

Φ:=E×Π×E.\Phi := E \times \Pi \times E.

Its elements are the predication cells — triples (s,p,o)(s, p, o) at which a predication valuation assigns a value in the predication fiber algebra ApA_p.

The cell set makes explicit what space the framework’s valuations are functions on: not the entities alone, not the predicates alone, but the full subject-predicate-object index. A valuation is a section over Φ\Phi — assigning a bilattice value to every triple — and it is this sectional structure that the rest of the framework’s machinery (the predicate category, the predication fibration) operates on.

item.title: Residuated lattice
item.description:
A commutative residuated lattice is an algebra L=⟨L,∨,∧,⋅,→,1⟩\mathbf{L} = \langle L, \vee, \wedge, \cdot, \to, 1 \rangle in which ⟨L,∨,∧⟩\langle L, \vee, \wedge \rangle is a lattice, ⟨L,⋅,1⟩\langle L, \cdot, 1 \rangle is a commutative monoid, and the binary operations ⋅\cdot (the fusion product) and →\to (the residual) satisfy the residuation adjunction:

x⋅y≤z  ⟺  x≤y→zx \cdot y \leq z \iff x \leq y \to z

for all x,y,z∈Lx, y, z \in L.

A residuated lattice is integral when 11 is the top element of the lattice, and bounded when a bottom element 00 exists.

item.title: Settling bound
item.description:
A settling bound is an ordinal λ\lambda that bounds the settling index of a family of predication valuations. Settling steps τ\tau range over τ<λ\tau < \lambda; the family is (vτ)τ<λ(v_\tau)_{\tau < \lambda}.

The settling bound is typically ω\omega — the regime of countable settlings, in which finite-convergence theorems live — but may be any fixed ordinal. Bounding by a fixed λ\lambda avoids the size-theoretic issues of indexing by the class of all ordinals while retaining the flexibility to support transfinite settlings, in which closure operators take strictly more than ω\omega-many iterations to reach quiescence.

item.title: Settling monotonicity
item.description:
Settling monotonicity is the condition on a settle-indexed family v=(vτ)τ<λv = (v_\tau)_{\tau < \lambda} of predication valuations — indexed by a settling bound λ\lambda — that values grow monotonically in the knowledge order: for all σ≤τ<λ\sigma \leq \tau < \lambda and every predicate pp,

vσ(p)≤kvτ(p)v_\sigma(p) \leq_k v_\tau(p)

pointwise in the predication fiber algebra ApA_p, where ≤k\leq_k is the knowledge order.

In ordinary language: as settling proceeds, the value at every cell only ever rises in the knowledge order. It may shift in truth-axis direction (toward affirmation, toward denial, toward paraconsistent both-directions); but it never loses evidence. What was witnessed remains witnessed.

item.title: Spatiotemporal knowledge presheaf
item.description:
Fix a residuated lattice L\mathbf{L}, a predicate category C\mathcal{C}, an entity set EE, and a settling bound λ\lambda.

A spatiotemporal knowledge presheaf over (L,C,E,λ)(\mathbf{L}, \mathcal{C}, E, \lambda) is a family v=(vτ)τ<λv = (v_\tau)_{\tau < \lambda} in which:

  • each vτv_\tau is a predication valuation, assigning to every predicate p∈Cp \in \mathcal{C} an element vτ(p)∈Apv_\tau(p) \in A_p of the predication fiber algebra at pp;
  • the family satisfies settling monotonicity — for all σ≤τ<λ\sigma \leq \tau < \lambda and every pp,
vσ(p)≤kvτ(p)v_\sigma(p) \leq_k v_\tau(p)

pointwise in ApA_p, where ≤k\leq_k is the knowledge order.

¶What it encodes

A spatiotemporal knowledge presheaf is the framework’s central temporal-evidence object. It packages, into a single coherent structure:

item.title: Twist-product fusion
item.description:
The twist-product fusion is a binary operation ⊙\odot on the carrier L×LL \times L of the twist product L⋈\mathbf{L}^\bowtie over an integral commutative residuated lattice L=⟨L,∨,∧,⋅,→,1⟩\mathbf{L} = \langle L, \vee, \wedge, \cdot, \to, 1 \rangle:

⟨x,y⟩⊙⟨z,v⟩:=⟨x⋅z, (x→v)∧(z→y)⟩.\langle x, y \rangle \odot \langle z, v \rangle := \langle x \cdot z,\ (x \to v) \wedge (z \to y) \rangle.

The first component is the monoid product of positive-evidence components: affirming evidence multiplies directly. The second component cross-tests each side’s positive evidence against the other side’s negative evidence: the x→vx \to v term measures how much the first side’s affirmation is challenged by the second side’s denial, and the z→yz \to y term symmetrically.

item.title: Twist-product right residual
item.description:
The twist-product right residual is a binary operation ⇒\Rightarrow on the carrier L×LL \times L of the twist product L⋈\mathbf{L}^\bowtie over an integral commutative residuated lattice L=⟨L,∨,∧,⋅,→,1⟩\mathbf{L} = \langle L, \vee, \wedge, \cdot, \to, 1 \rangle:

⟨x,y⟩⇒⟨z,v⟩:=⟨(x→z)∧(v→y), x⋅v⟩.\langle x, y \rangle \Rightarrow \langle z, v \rangle := \langle (x \to z) \wedge (v \to y),\ x \cdot v \rangle.

The first component tests implication in both directions of the residuated-lattice structure: the x→zx \to z term measures how much the antecedent’s affirmation suffices for the consequent’s affirmation, and the v→yv \to y term measures how much the consequent’s denial suffices for the antecedent’s denial (the contrapositive direction). The second component collects the product of the antecedent’s affirming evidence with the consequent’s denying evidence — the “support for refutation” carried by the implication.

item.title: Bilattice
item.description:
In voidmath, a bilattice is the algebraic structure of values that can be held at a predication cell. It is what makes voidmath’s settlement four-cornered rather than bivalent: where classical logic gives two truth values and intuitionistic logic gives one truth direction with constructive grounding, the voidmath bilattice gives two parallel orders that compose to a four-corner shape.

The two parallel orders are:

  • The knowledge order ≤k\leq_k — measures how much evidence has accumulated, with bottom ⊥\bot (no evidence) and top ⊤\top (full evidence in both directions). The math-side counterpart of the absurd ↔ determination axis in the relationality framework.
  • The truth order ≤t\leq_t — measures direction of evidence (affirming vs denying), with bottom f\mathbf{f} (false) and top t\mathbf{t} (true). The math-side counterpart of the false ↔ true axis.

The two orders are independent, and their joint extremes give the four corners of settlement: t\mathbf{t} (true-only), f\mathbf{f} (false-only), ⊤\top (both), ⊥\bot (neither).

item.title: Knowledge order
item.description: The knowledge order ≤k\leq_k is one of the two parallel orders on a bilattice. It ranges over evidential content: a value sits higher in ≤k\leq_k when more evidence has accumulated, regardless of whether the evidence is affirming or denying. When the bilattice is bounded, the knowledge order has bottom ⊥\bot (no information at all) and top ⊤\top (full evidence in both directions). The knowledge order is independent of the truth order, which measures direction rather than amount.

item.title: Predicate category
item.description:
A predicate category, denoted C\mathcal{C}, is a small category whose objects are predicates and whose morphisms record the structural relationships between them. The morphisms are not freely chosen: under a Sym(E)\mathrm{Sym}(E)-equivariance hypothesis on the entity set EE (with ∣E∣≥3|E| \geq 3), every natural map between predication fiber algebras is generated by exactly four canonical classes. That the generator set is exactly four classes is a theorem about equivariance, not a stipulation.

The four-generator structure is the mathematical articulation of the structural lopsidedness that the relationality framework commits to philosophically. It is where the four-cornered shape of predication — the four positions a settlement can occupy across the knowledge and truth orders — gets its canonical content.

item.title: Predication
item.description: Predication is the assignment of a value at a directed triple — a subject, a predicate, and an object. It is the mathematical primitive that relating in the relationality framework becomes when expressed in the standard vocabulary of categorical logic and bilattice-valued algebra. The two terms name the same activity from different sides: relating is the activity in the philosophical commitment that relations are ontologically prior to entities; predication is the formal structure that activity has when written down with mathematics that already exists.

item.title: Predication cell
item.description:
A predication cell is an ordered triple ϕ=(s,p,o)\phi = (s, p, o) where ss is a subject (an entity), pp is a predicate, and oo is an object (also an entity). The triple records the proposition predicate pp holds of subject ss in relation to object oo. Predication cells are the elements of the predication cell set Φ=E×Π×E\Phi = E \times \Pi \times E, where EE is a set of entities and Π\Pi is a set of predicates.

The cell is the smallest unit at which a predication valuation can assign a value. The value lives in a residuated bilattice L⋈\mathbf{L}^\bowtie associated to the predicate; the cell is where the value is assigned, the value is what is assigned. Predication cells are not relations in their own right — they are the directed triadic shapes that relations take when written out.

item.title: Predication fiber algebra
item.description:
The predication fiber algebra at a predicate pp, denoted ApA_p, is the algebra of values that can be held at every predication cell with predicate coordinate fixed at pp. Concretely, it is the pointwise twist product of a residuated lattice L\mathbf{L} over the entity-pair index E×EE \times E:

Ap:=L⋈ E×E.A_p := \mathbf{L}^{\bowtie\, E \times E}.

An element of ApA_p is a function E×E→L×LE \times E \to L \times L — at each subject–object pair (s,o)(s, o), a value v(s,o)∈L⋈v(s, o) \in \mathbf{L}^\bowtie in the bilattice. As an abstract algebra, ApA_p is the same for every predicate; the subscript pp records which predicate’s values are being held, not a difference in the algebra’s structure.

item.title: Predication fibration
item.description:
A predication fibration is a cloven Grothendieck fibration

p:E↠Cp : \mathcal{E} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{C}

over a predicate category C\mathcal{C}, whose fibers are predication fiber algebras and whose reindexing along each of the four generator classes acts cellwise by the manipulation of the entity-pair index E×EE \times E specified by that generator. It is the fibrational layer of the framework — the structure that ties predicates (objects of C\mathcal{C}) to the values held at their cells (elements of the fibers Ep≅Ap\mathcal{E}_p \cong A_p) in a way that makes substitution and reindexing well-behaved.

item.title: Predication hyperdoctrine
item.description:
A predication hyperdoctrine is a predication fibration that additionally supports four classes of structure, each a property in the Kelly–Lack sense (present-or-not, no choice involved, unique up to unique iso when present). It is the predicate-logical layer of the framework — the structure at which substitution, quantification, equality, and comprehension all appear, and at which predication can be reasoned about as a logic in the Lawvere–Seely sense.

¶The four conditions

(B1) Quantifier adjoints. For every morphism f:q→pf : q \to p in the predicate category C\mathcal{C}, the reindexing f∗:Ep→Eqf^* : \mathcal{E}_p \to \mathcal{E}_q has both a left adjoint and a right adjoint in each of the two parallel orders. In the knowledge order:

item.title: Predication valuation
item.description:
A predication valuation is a function vv that assigns to each predicate pp an element v(p)∈Apv(p) \in A_p of the predication fiber algebra at pp. This determines a value at each predication cell (s,p,o)(s, p, o): namely v(p)(s,o)∈L⋈v(p)(s, o) \in \mathbf{L}^\bowtie, a position in the settlement space.

The valuation is the content of a predication system at a moment. The cells are where values can sit; the fiber algebras are what values can be; the valuation is which values are actually being held. A system with a fixed set of entities and predicates can hold different valuations — the valuation is the dynamic data, not the static structure.

item.title: Settlement
item.description: In voidmath, settlement names the four-cornered joint structure within which every predication value sits. It is not a single position but a position in a two-dimensional space — the space whose two axes are the truth order (≤t\leq_t, running from f\mathbf{f} to t\mathbf{t}) and the knowledge order (≤k\leq_k, running from ⊥\bot to ⊤\top). The four corners of this space are the extremal positions a value can occupy in the bilattice; the interior is the mass of partial settlements that constitute most actual valuations.

item.title: Truth order
item.description:
The truth order ≤t\leq_t is one of the two parallel orders on a bilattice. It ranges over the direction of evidence: a value sits higher in ≤t\leq_t when its evidential content tilts toward affirmation rather than denial. When the bilattice is bounded, the truth order has bottom f\mathbf{f} (false) and top t\mathbf{t} (true). The truth order is independent of the knowledge order, which measures amount rather than direction.

¶In a twist product

Inside a twist product L1⋈L2\mathbf{L}_1 \bowtie \mathbf{L}_2, the truth order is defined component-asymmetrically:

item.title: Twist product
item.description:
The twist product in voidmath is the construction that produces the voidmath bilattice from a residuated lattice. Given a residuated lattice L\mathbf{L} with order ≤\leq, the twist product L⋈\mathbf{L}^\bowtie — equivalently written L⋈L\mathbf{L} \bowtie \mathbf{L} — has carrier set L×LL \times L, with elements interpreted as pairs ⟨a,b⟩\langle a, b \rangle where:

  • a∈La \in L is the affirming-evidence component
  • b∈Lb \in L is the denying-evidence component

The two-component structure is what gives the bilattice its two parallel orders: the knowledge order and the truth order. Without the pair structure there is no second axis; the twist product is the construction that supplies it.
